Stack

Stack is a data structure with Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) property. Each operation adds/removes from the top of the stack.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity: O(1) for push, pop, peek
  • Space Complexity: O(n) where n is the number of elements
  • Core Principle: Last element added is the first one removed
  • Use Case: Problems involving order reversal, pattern matching, or maintaining context

Quick Decision Guide

Problem Type Pattern Key Idea Examples
Find next greater/smaller element Monotonic Stack Maintain increasing/decreasing order LC 496, 503, 739
Remove adjacent duplicates Stack with Pair [element, count] Track counts, pop when k reached LC 1047, 1209, 1544
Decode strings with brackets Stack with Count Use pairs for nested repetitions LC 394, 726
Arithmetic expressions Stack with Operators Handle precedence and evaluation LC 224, 227
Remove k digits for min number Greedy + Monotonic Pop larger digits when beneficial LC 402
Lexicographically smallest with duplicates Monotonic + Last Occurrence Greedy removal with “appears later” check LC 316, 1081
Streaming/online frequency Stack with Span Pairs Accumulate counts in pairs LC 901
FIFO from LIFO Two Stacks Use input/output stacks for queue LC 232

How to read: Find your problem goal in the leftmost column, then use the pattern and examples as a starting point.

  • Ref
    • fuck-Algorithm - single stack
    • fuck-Algorithm - implement array via stack / stack via array

Important Tips

  • Monotonic Stack: Critical pattern for problems involving “next greater/smaller” — check if the pattern requires increasing or decreasing order
  • Stack with Pair: For adjacent duplicate removal or nested counting problems, store [element, count] pairs
  • Greedy Removal: Some problems benefit from greedily removing elements while maintaining an invariant

Video References

  • Stack Fundamentals

  • Java Stack
    • Low level : Array

0-1) Types

  • Single stack
  • Build queue via stack
    • LC 232 (use 2 stack)
  • Build stack via queue
  • Stack with Pair (char, count)
    • Store [element, count] pairs instead of raw elements
    • LC 1047 (k=2 special case, simple pop)
    • LC 1209 (k consecutive duplicates removal)
    • LC 1544 (Make The String Great)
    • LC 394 (Decode String, stack with count for repetition)
    • LC 726 (Number of Atoms)

0-2) Pattern

1# python
2# LC 739, LC 503 - Find next `big number`
3# ...
4stack = [] # [[idx, val]]
5for i, val in enumerate(len(tmp)):
6    while stack and stack[-1][1] < val:
7        _idx, _val = stack.pop(-1)
8        res[tmp[_idx]] = i - _idx
9    stack.append([i, val]) 
10# ...

1// java
2// LC 739
3for (int j = 0; j < temperatures.length; j++){
4    int x = temperatures[j];
5    /**
6     *  NOTE !!!
7     *   1) while loop
8     *   2) stack is NOT empty
9     *   3) cache temperature smaller than current temperature
10     *
11     *   st.peek().get(0) is cached temperature
12     */
13    while (!st.isEmpty() && st.peek().get(0) < x){
14        /**
15         *  st.peek().get(1) is idx
16         *
17         */
18        nextGreater[st.peek().get(1)] = j - st.peek().get(1);
19        st.pop();
20    }
  • monotonic stack (increasing)
1// java
2// LC 239
3// LC 496
4// ...
5
6// Traverse the array from right to left
7for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
8    // Maintain a decreasing monotonic stack
9    /** NOTE !!! below */
10    while (!stack.isEmpty() && nums[stack.peek()] <= nums[i]) {
11        stack.pop();  // Pop elements from the stack that are smaller or equal to the current element
12    }
13    
14    // If stack is not empty, the next greater element is at the top of the stack
15    if (!stack.isEmpty()) {
16        result[i] = nums[stack.peek()];
17    }
18    
19    // Push the current element's index onto the stack
20    stack.push(i);
21}
22
23// ...
  • Stack with Pair [element, count] (adjacent duplicate removal with k)
1Core Idea:
2  - Instead of pushing raw elements, push [element, count] pairs
3  - When current element == stack top element: increment count
4  - When count reaches k: pop (remove k consecutive duplicates)
5  - Handles partial sequences naturally (e.g., "aa" waiting for 3rd 'a')
6
7When to Use:
8  - Remove k consecutive/adjacent equal elements (k >= 2)
9  - Track both identity AND frequency of consecutive runs
10  - Need compressed representation of the string/array
11  - LC 1047 is k=2 special case (simple pop, no count needed)
12
13Similar LC:
14  - LC 1047  Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String (k=2)
15  - LC 1209  Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II (general k)
16  - LC 1544  Make The String Great (adjacent opposite-case removal)
17  - LC 394   Decode String (stack with count for repetition)
18  - LC 726   Number of Atoms (nested count tracking)

1# python - Stack Pair pattern
2# LC 1209
3stack = [['#', 0]]  # sentinel to avoid empty-check
4for c in s:
5    if stack[-1][0] == c:
6        stack[-1][1] += 1
7        if stack[-1][1] == k:
8            stack.pop()
9    else:
10        stack.append([c, 1])
11return ''.join(c * cnt for c, cnt in stack)

1// java - Stack Pair pattern
2// LC 1209
3Stack<int[]> stack = new Stack<>(); // {char_as_int, count}
4for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
5    if (!stack.isEmpty() && stack.peek()[0] == c) {
6        stack.peek()[1]++;
7        if (stack.peek()[1] == k) {
8            stack.pop();
9        }
10    } else {
11        stack.push(new int[]{c, 1});
12    }
13}
14StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
15for (int[] pair : stack) {
16    for (int i = 0; i < pair[1]; i++) {
17        sb.append((char) pair[0]);
18    }
19}
20return sb.toString();
  • monotonic stack (decreasing)
1
2// java
3for (int j = 0; j < temperatures.length; j++) {
4    int x = temperatures[j];
5    /**
6     *  NOTE !!!
7     *   1) while loop
8     *   2) stack is NOT empty
9     *   3) cache temperature greater than current temperature
10     *
11     *   st.peek().get(0) is cached temperature
12     */
13     /** NOTE !!! below */
14    while (!st.isEmpty() && st.peek().get(0) > x) {
15        /**
16         *  st.peek().get(1) is idx
17         *
18         */
19        nextGreater[st.peek().get(1)] = j - st.peek().get(1);
20        st.pop();
21    }
22    
23    // Push the current temperature and its index to the stack
24    st.push(new Pair<>(x, j));
25}
  • monotonic stack (greedy removal pattern - Remove K Digits)
1// java
2// LC 402 Remove K Digits
3// Pattern: Maintain monotonic increasing stack by greedily removing larger digits
4
5Deque<Character> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
6
7for (int i = 0; i < num.length(); i++) {
8    char digit = num.charAt(i);
9
10    /**
11     * NOTE !!!
12     * Greedy removal: while we can still remove digits (k > 0)
13     * and current digit is smaller than stack top,
14     * pop the larger digit to make the number smaller
15     */
16    while (k > 0 && !stack.isEmpty() && stack.peekLast() > digit) {
17        stack.removeLast();
18        k--;
19    }
20    stack.addLast(digit);
21}
22
23// If k > 0, remove remaining digits from the end
24while (k > 0) {
25    stack.removeLast();
26    k--;
27}
28
29// Remove leading zeros
30StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
31boolean leadingZero = true;
32while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
33    char c = stack.removeFirst();
34    if (leadingZero && c == '0')
35        continue;
36    leadingZero = false;
37    sb.append(c);
38}
39
40return sb.length() == 0 ? "0" : sb.toString();
  • monotonic stack (lexicographical order with deduplication - Remove Duplicate Letters)
1// java
2// LC 316 Remove Duplicate Letters (same as LC 1081)
3// Pattern: Monotonic increasing stack with "will appear later" check for lexicographical smallest result
4
5/**
6 * Key differences from basic greedy removal:
7 * 1. Track which characters are already in result (inStack/seen)
8 * 2. Only remove if character will appear again later (lastOccurrence)
9 * 3. Each character must appear exactly once
10 */
11
12// Step 1: Store the LAST index where each character appears
13int[] lastOccurrence = new int[26];
14for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
15    lastOccurrence[s.charAt(i) - 'a'] = i;
16}
17
18Stack<Character> stack = new Stack<>();
19// Use boolean array for O(1) "contains" check
20boolean[] inStack = new boolean[26];
21
22for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++) {
23    char c = s.charAt(i);
24
25    // If character already in stack, skip it (each char appears once)
26    if (inStack[c - 'a'])
27        continue;
28
29    /**
30     * NOTE !!! MONO STACK LOGIC with "will appear later" check
31     *
32     * We can safely remove stack top if ALL conditions met:
33     * 0. Stack is NOT empty
34     * 1. Stack top is BIGGER than current char (for lexicographical order)
35     * 2. Stack top will appear AGAIN LATER (lastOccurrence[stack.peek()] > i)
36     *
37     * This ensures we get the lexicographically smallest result
38     */
39    while (!stack.isEmpty() && stack.peek() > c && lastOccurrence[stack.peek() - 'a'] > i) {
40        char removed = stack.pop();
41        inStack[removed - 'a'] = false;  // Mark as not in stack
42    }
43
44    stack.push(c);
45    inStack[c - 'a'] = true;  // Mark as in stack
46}
47
48// Build result from stack
49StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
50for (char c : stack) {
51    sb.append(c);
52}
53return sb.toString();

Explanation of “will appear later” logic:

1/**
2 * lastOccurrence array tracks the LAST index of each character
3 *
4 * Example: s = "cbacdcbc"
5 *
6 * lastOccurrence['c' - 'a'] = 7  (last 'c' at index 7)
7 * lastOccurrence['b' - 'a'] = 6  (last 'b' at index 6)
8 * lastOccurrence['a' - 'a'] = 2  (last 'a' at index 2)
9 * lastOccurrence['d' - 'a'] = 4  (last 'd' at index 4)
10 *
11 * When at index i = 1 (char 'b'):
12 * - Stack has 'c', current char is 'b'
13 * - 'c' > 'b' (can potentially remove 'c')
14 * - lastOccurrence['c'] = 7 > 1 (YES, 'c' appears later)
15 * - Safe to remove 'c' and add 'b' first
16 *
17 * When at index i = 2 (char 'a'):
18 * - Stack has 'b', current char is 'a'
19 * - 'b' > 'a' (can potentially remove 'b')
20 * - lastOccurrence['b'] = 6 > 2 (YES, 'b' appears later)
21 * - Safe to remove 'b' and add 'a' first
22 *
23 * Result: "acdb" (lexicographically smallest)
24 */
  • monotonic stack (span accumulation pattern - Online Stock Span)
1// java
2// LC 901 Online Stock Span
3// Pattern: Monotonic decreasing stack with span accumulation for streaming data
4
5Deque<int[]> stack = new ArrayDeque<>(); // {price, span}
6
7public int next(int price) {
8    int span = 1; // Today always counts
9
10    /**
11     * NOTE !!!
12     * Pop all smaller/equal prices and accumulate their spans
13     * This gives us the count of consecutive days with price <= current
14     */
15    while (!stack.isEmpty() && stack.peek()[0] <= price) {
16        span += stack.pop()[1]; // Absorb previous span
17    }
18
19    stack.push(new int[] { price, span });
20    return span;
21}
22
23/**
24 * Key differences from other monotonic stack patterns:
25 * 1. Streaming/online: processes data one at a time (not batch)
26 * 2. Span accumulation: accumulates counts rather than finding next element
27 * 3. Stateful: maintains stack across multiple calls
28 * 4. Stack stores pairs: [value, accumulated_count]
29 */
  • Implement Queue using Stacks
1// java
2
3// LC 232
4// V3
5// https://leetcode.com/problems/implement-queue-using-stacks/solutions/6579732/video-simple-solution-by-niits-sqaw/
6// IDEA: 2 stack
7class MyQueue_3{
8    private Stack<Integer> input;
9    private Stack<Integer> output;
10
11    public MyQueue_3() {
12        input = new Stack<>();
13        output = new Stack<>();
14    }
15
16    public void push(int x) {
17        input.push(x);
18    }
19
20    public int pop() {
21        /**
22         *  NOTE !!!
23         *
24         *  1)  before calling pop() directly,
25         *      we firstly call `peak()`
26         *      purpose:
27         *        reset / reassign elements at `output` stack,
28         *        so we can have the element in `queue ordering` in `output` stack
29         *
30         *  2) peak() return an integer, but it DOES NOT terminate the pop() execution
31         *     since the `peek()` method is called and NOT assign its result to any object,
32         *     then the `output.pop();` code is executed and return as result
33         */
34        peek();
35        return output.pop();
36    }
37
38    public int peek() {
39        if (output.isEmpty()) {
40            while (!input.isEmpty()) {
41                output.push(input.pop());
42            }
43        }
44        return output.peek();
45    }
46
47    public boolean empty() {
48        return input.isEmpty() && output.isEmpty();
49    }
50}

1) Core Patterns

1-1) Basic Stack Operations

Stack push (insert):

1// Java
2Stack<Integer> stack = new Stack<>();
3stack.push(element);  // O(1)

1# Python
2stack = []
3stack.append(element)  # O(1)

Stack pop (remove top):

1// Java
2int top = stack.pop();  // O(1), throws if empty

1# Python
2top = stack.pop()  # O(1), raises if empty

Stack peek (view top):

1// Java
2int top = stack.peek();  // O(1), throws if empty
3if (!stack.isEmpty()) {
4    top = stack.peek();
5}

1# Python
2top = stack[-1]  # O(1), raises if empty
3if stack:
4    top = stack[-1]

1-2) Monotonic Stack: Next Greater Element

1// java
2// LC 496
3  // V0
4    // IDEA : STACK
5    // https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=68a1Dc_qVq4
6    /** NOTE !!!
7     *
8     *  nums1 is "sub set" of nums2,
9     *  so all elements in nums1 are in nums2 as well
10     *  and in order to find next greater element in nums1 reference nums2
11     *  -> ACTUALLY we only need to check nums2
12     *  -> then append result per element in nums1
13     */
14    /**
15     *
16     *  Example 1)
17     *
18     *  nums1 = [4,1,2]
19     *  nums2 = [1,3,4,2]
20     *           x
21     *             x
22     *               x
23     *                 x
24     *  st = [1]
25     *  st = [3]  map : {1:3}
26     *  st = [4], map : {1:3, 3:4}
27     *  st = [], map : {1:3, 3:4}
28     *
29     *  so, res = [-1, 3, -1]
30     *
31     *
32     *  Example 2)
33     *
34     *   nums1 = [1,3,5,2,4]
35     *   nums2 = [6,5,4,3,2,1,7]
36     *            x
37     *              x
38     *               x
39     *                 x
40     *                   x
41     *                     x
42     *                       x
43     *                         x
44     *
45     *  st = [6], map :{}
46     *  st = [6,5],  map :{}
47     *  ..
48     *
49     *  st = [6,5,4,3,2,1], map = {}
50     *  st = [], map = {6:7, 5:7,4:7,3:7,2:7,1:7}
51     *
52     */
53    public int[] nextGreaterElement(int[] nums1, int[] nums2) {
54
55        if (nums1.length == 1 && nums2.length == 1){
56            return new int[]{-1};
57        }
58
59        /**
60         *  NOTE !!!
61         *  we use map " collect next greater element"
62         *  map definition :  {element, next-greater-element}
63         */
64        Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
65        Stack<Integer> st = new Stack<>();
66
67        for (int x : nums2){
68            /**
69             *  NOTE !!!
70             *   1) use while loop
71             *   2) while stack is NOT null and stack "top" element is smaller than current element (x) is nums2
72             *
73             *   -> found "next greater element", so update map
74             */
75            while(!st.isEmpty() && st.peek() < x){
76                int cur = st.pop();
77                map.put(cur, x);
78            }
79            /** NOTE !!! if not feat above condition, we put element to stack */
80            st.add(x);
81        }
82
83        //System.out.println("map = " + map);
84        int[] res = new int[nums1.length];
85        // fill with -1 for element without next greater element
86        Arrays.fill(res, -1);
87        for (int j = 0; j < nums1.length; j++){
88            if(map.containsKey(nums1[j])){
89                res[j] = map.get(nums1[j]);
90            }
91        }
92
93        //System.out.println("res = " + res);
94        return res;
95    }

1-1-2-2) next greater element 2

1# V0
2# IDEA : LC 739, LC 503
3class Solution(object):
4    def nextGreaterElements(self, nums):
5        # edge case
6        if not nums:
7            return
8        _len = len(nums)
9        # note : we init res as [-1] * _len
10        res = [-1] * _len
11        # note : we use "nums = 2 * nums" to simuldate "circular array"
12        nums = 2 * nums
13        stack = [] # [[idx, val]]
14        for idx, val in enumerate(nums):
15            while stack and stack[-1][1] < val:
16                _idx, _val = stack.pop(-1)
17                """
18                NOTE !!!
19                    -> we get remainder via "_idx % _len" for handling idx issue
20                      (since we made nums = 2 * nums earlier)
21                """
22                res[_idx % _len] = val
23            stack.append([idx, val])
24        return res

1-1-3) get non balanced String

1# LC 1963. Minimum Number of Swaps to Make the String Balanced
2
3# NOTE !!! below trick will ONLY collect not Balanced ], [
4#          -> e.g. "]][[" or "]]][[["
5 
6s = "]]][[["
7stack = []
8for i in range(len(s)):
9    # NOTE HERE !!!
10    if stack and s[i] == "]":
11        stack.pop(-1)
12    else:
13        stack.append()
14print (stack)

1-1-4) deal with pre num, pre string

1# LC 227, 394
2# ...
3for i, each in enumerate(s):
4    # ...
5    if i == len(s) - 1 or each in "+-*/":
6        if pre_op == "+":
7            # ...
8        elif pre_op == "-":
9            # ...
10        elif pre_op == "*":
11            # ...
12        elif pre_op == "/":
13            # ...
14        """
15        NOTE this !!!
16        """
17        pre_op = each
18        num = 0
19# ...

2) LeetCode Examples

2-1) Decode String

1# LC 394 Decode String
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK
4# NOTE : treat before cases separately
5#        1) isdigit
6#        2) isalpha
7#        3) "["
8#        4) "]"
9# and define num = 0 for dealing with "100a[b]", "10abc" cases
10class Solution:
11    def decodeString(self, s):
12        num = 0
13        string = ''
14        stack = []
15        """
16        NOTE : we deal with 4 cases
17            1) digit
18            2) "["
19            3) alphabet
20            4) "]"
21
22        NOTE :
23            we use pre_num, pre_string for dealing with previous result
24        """
25        for c in s:
26            # case 1) : digit
27            if c.isdigit():
28                num = num*10 + int(c)
29            # case 2) : "["
30            elif c == "[":
31                stack.append(string)
32                stack.append(num)
33                string = ''
34                num = 0
35            # case 3) : alphabet
36            elif c.isalpha():
37                string += c
38            # case 4) "]"
39            elif c == ']':
40                pre_num = stack.pop()
41                pre_string = stack.pop()
42                string = pre_string + pre_num * string
43        return string

1// java
2// LC 394 Decode String
3
4/**
5 * Problem: Given an encoded string, return its decoded string.
6 *
7 * Encoding rule: k[encoded_string] means repeat encoded_string k times
8 *
9 * Examples:
10 * - "3[a]2[bc]" → "aaabcbc"
11 * - "3[a2[c]]" → "accaccacc"
12 * - "2[abc]3[cd]ef" → "abcabccdcdcdef"
13 *
14 * Key Insight:
15 * - Use stack to handle nested brackets
16 * - Process 4 cases: digit, '[', letter, ']'
17 * - Build number incrementally (e.g., "100" = 1*10 + 0*10 + 0)
18 * - On ']': pop count and previous string, build result
19 *
20 * Time: O(maxK * N) where maxK is max k value and N is length of decoded string
21 * Space: O(N) for the stack
22 */
23
24// V0
25// IDEA: STACK + 4 CASES (digit, '[', letter, ']')
26public String decodeString(String s) {
27    if (s == null || s.length() == 0) {
28        return "";
29    }
30
31    /**
32     * NOTE !!!
33     * Stack stores alternating pattern:
34     * - String (previous accumulated string)
35     * - Integer (repeat count)
36     * - String (next accumulated string)
37     * - Integer (next repeat count)
38     * ...
39     *
40     * Example for "3[a2[c]]":
41     * When processing '2[c]':
42     *   Stack bottom: ["", 3, "a", 2] Stack top
43     */
44    Stack<Object> stack = new Stack<>();
45
46    int num = 0;              // Current number being built
47    String currentString = ""; // Current string being built
48
49    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
50
51        /**
52         * Case 1: Digit
53         * Build multi-digit numbers (e.g., "100")
54         */
55        if (Character.isDigit(c)) {
56            num = num * 10 + (c - '0');
57        }
58
59        /**
60         * Case 2: '['
61         * Push current string and number to stack
62         * Reset for new nested level
63         */
64        else if (c == '[') {
65            // Push current string first, then number
66            stack.push(currentString);
67            stack.push(num);
68
69            // Reset for new level
70            currentString = "";
71            num = 0;
72        }
73
74        /**
75         * Case 3: Letter
76         * Append to current string
77         */
78        else if (Character.isLetter(c)) {
79            currentString += c;
80        }
81
82        /**
83         * Case 4: ']'
84         * Pop count and previous string
85         * Build repeated string and concatenate
86         */
87        else if (c == ']') {
88            // Pop in reverse order of push
89            int repeatCount = (int) stack.pop();
90            String prevString = (String) stack.pop();
91
92            /**
93             * NOTE !!!
94             * Repeat current string repeatCount times
95             * Then prepend previous string
96             */
97            StringBuilder temp = new StringBuilder(prevString);
98            for (int i = 0; i < repeatCount; i++) {
99                temp.append(currentString);
100            }
101
102            currentString = temp.toString();
103        }
104    }
105
106    return currentString;
107}
108
109/**
110 * Example Walkthrough: s = "3[a2[c]]"
111 *
112 * Step 1: c='3' (digit)
113 *   num = 3
114 *
115 * Step 2: c='[' (open bracket)
116 *   stack.push("") → stack: [""]
117 *   stack.push(3)  → stack: ["", 3]
118 *   currentString = "", num = 0
119 *
120 * Step 3: c='a' (letter)
121 *   currentString = "a"
122 *
123 * Step 4: c='2' (digit)
124 *   num = 2
125 *
126 * Step 5: c='[' (open bracket)
127 *   stack.push("a") → stack: ["", 3, "a"]
128 *   stack.push(2)   → stack: ["", 3, "a", 2]
129 *   currentString = "", num = 0
130 *
131 * Step 6: c='c' (letter)
132 *   currentString = "c"
133 *
134 * Step 7: c=']' (close bracket)
135 *   repeatCount = stack.pop() = 2
136 *   prevString = stack.pop() = "a"
137 *   temp = "a" + "c" * 2 = "acc"
138 *   currentString = "acc"
139 *   stack: ["", 3]
140 *
141 * Step 8: c=']' (close bracket)
142 *   repeatCount = stack.pop() = 3
143 *   prevString = stack.pop() = ""
144 *   temp = "" + "acc" * 3 = "accaccacc"
145 *   currentString = "accaccacc"
146 *   stack: []
147 *
148 * Result: "accaccacc"
149 */
150
151// V1
152// IDEA: STACK with separate stacks for counts and strings (cleaner approach)
153public String decodeString_v1(String s) {
154    Stack<Integer> countStack = new Stack<>();
155    Stack<String> stringStack = new Stack<>();
156
157    String currentString = "";
158    int num = 0;
159
160    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
161        if (Character.isDigit(c)) {
162            num = num * 10 + (c - '0');
163        }
164        else if (c == '[') {
165            // Push current state to stacks
166            countStack.push(num);
167            stringStack.push(currentString);
168
169            // Reset for nested level
170            currentString = "";
171            num = 0;
172        }
173        else if (c == ']') {
174            // Build decoded string for this level
175            int repeatCount = countStack.pop();
176            StringBuilder temp = new StringBuilder(stringStack.pop());
177
178            for (int i = 0; i < repeatCount; i++) {
179                temp.append(currentString);
180            }
181
182            currentString = temp.toString();
183        }
184        else {
185            // Letter
186            currentString += c;
187        }
188    }
189
190    return currentString;
191}
192
193/**
194 * Common Mistakes:
195 *
196 * 1. Not handling multi-digit numbers (e.g., "100[a]")
197 *    ✗ num = c - '0'
198 *    ✓ num = num * 10 + (c - '0')
199 *
200 * 2. Wrong stack push/pop order
201 *    ✗ push(num, string) → pop(string, num)  // Wrong!
202 *    ✓ push(string, num) → pop(num, string)  // Correct LIFO
203 *
204 * 3. Forgetting to reset num and currentString after '['
205 *    ✗ Only reset one of them
206 *    ✓ Reset both: num = 0; currentString = "";
207 *
208 * 4. Not handling strings outside brackets (e.g., "2[abc]3[cd]ef")
209 *    ✓ Continue building currentString for letters outside brackets
210 *
211 * 5. Using Stack<Object> without proper casting
212 *    ✓ Use separate stacks (countStack, stringStack) for type safety
213 */
214
215/**
216 * Interview Tips:
217 *
218 * 1. Clarify constraints:
219 *    - Is input always valid? (no unmatched brackets)
220 *    - Max value of k? (affects overflow considerations)
221 *
222 * 2. Edge cases to test:
223 *    - No brackets: "abc" → "abc"
224 *    - Nested brackets: "2[a2[b]]" → "abbabb"
225 *    - Multi-digit numbers: "100[a]"
226 *    - Mixed: "2[abc]3[cd]ef" → "abcabccdcdcdef"
227 *
228 * 3. Follow-up questions:
229 *    - What if string is invalid? (add validation)
230 *    - Can we decode in-place? (no, need stack for nesting)
231 *    - How to handle very large k values? (streaming approach)
232 */

2-2) Next Greater Element I

1# 496. Next Greater Element I
2
3# V0
4# IDEA : STACK (for + while loop)
5class Solution(object):
6    def nextGreaterElement(self, nums1, nums2):
7        # edge case
8        if not nums2 or (not nums1 and not nums2):
9            return nums1
10        res = []
11        # NOTE : the trick here (found as a flag)
12        found = False
13        for i in nums1:
14            #print ("i = " + str(i) + " res = " + str(res))
15            idx = nums2.index(i)
16            # start from "next" element in nums2
17            # here we init tmp _nums2
18            _nums2 = nums2[idx+1:]
19            # while loop keep pop _nums2 for finding the next bigger element
20            while _nums2:
21                tmp = _nums2.pop(0)
22                # if found, then append to res, and break the while loop directly
23                if tmp > i:
24                    found = True
25                    res.append(tmp)
26                    break
27            # if not found, we need to append -1 to res
28            if not found:
29                res.append(-1)
30            found = False
31        return res
32
33# V0
34# IDEA : double for loop (one of loops is INVERSE ORDERING) + case conditions op
35class Solution(object):
36    def nextGreaterElement(self, nums1, nums2):
37        res = [None for _ in range(len(nums1))]
38        tmp = []
39        for i in range(len(nums1)):
40            ### NOTE : from last idx to 0 idx. (Note the start and end idx)
41            for j in range(len(nums2)-1, -1, -1):
42                #print ("i = " + str(i) + " j = " + str(j) + " tmp = " + str(tmp))
43
44                # case 1) No "next greater element" found in nums2
45                if not tmp and nums2[j] == nums1[i]:
46                    res[i] = -1
47                    break
48                # case 2) found "next greater element" in nums2, keep inverse looping
49                elif nums2[j] > nums1[i]:
50                    tmp.append(nums2[j])
51                # case 3) already reach same element in nums2 (as nums1), pop "last" "next greater element", paste to res, break the loop
52                elif tmp and nums2[j] == nums1[i]:
53                    _tmp = tmp.pop(-1)
54                    res[i] = _tmp
55                    tmp = []
56                    break
57        return res

1// java
2// LC 496
3// V0
4// IDEA : STACK
5// https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=68a1Dc_qVq4
6/** NOTE !!!
7 *
8 *  nums1 is "sub set" of nums2,
9 *  so all elements in nums1 are in nums2 as well
10 *  and in order to find next greater element in nums1 reference nums2
11 *  -> ACTUALLY we only need to check nums2
12 *  -> then append result per element in nums1
13 */
14public int[] nextGreaterElement(int[] nums1, int[] nums2) {
15
16    if (nums1.length == 1 && nums2.length == 1){
17        return new int[]{-1};
18    }
19
20    /**
21     *  NOTE !!!
22     *  we use map " collect next greater element"
23     *  map definition :  {element, next-greater-element}
24     */
25    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
26    Stack<Integer> st = new Stack<>();
27
28    for (int x : nums2){
29        /**
30         *  NOTE !!!
31         *   1) use while loop
32         *   2) while stack is NOT null and stack "top" element is smaller than current element (x) is nums2
33         *
34         *   -> found "next greater element", so update map
35         */
36        while(!st.isEmpty() && st.peek() < x){
37            int cur = st.pop();
38            map.put(cur, x);
39        }
40        /** NOTE !!! if not feat above condition, we put element to stack */
41        st.add(x);
42    }
43
44    //System.out.println("map = " + map);
45    int[] res = new int[nums1.length];
46    // fill with -1 for element without next greater element
47    Arrays.fill(res, -1);
48    for (int j = 0; j < nums1.length; j++){
49        if(map.containsKey(nums1[j])){
50            res[j] = map.get(nums1[j]);
51        }
52    }
53
54    //System.out.println("res = " + res);
55    return res;
56}

2-3) Next Greater Element II

1# LC 503. Next Greater Element II
2
3# V0'
4# IDEA : LC 739
5class Solution(object):
6    def nextGreaterElements(self, nums):
7        # edge case
8        if not nums:
9            return
10        _len = len(nums)
11        # note : we init res as [-1] * _len
12        res = [-1] * _len
13        # note : we use "nums = 2 * nums" to simuldate "circular array"
14        nums = 2 * nums
15        stack = [] # [[idx, val]]
16        for idx, val in enumerate(nums):
17            while stack and stack[-1][1] < val:
18                _idx, _val = stack.pop(-1)
19                """
20                NOTE !!!
21                    -> we get remainder via "_idx % _len" for handling idx issue
22                      (since we made nums = 2 * nums earlier)
23                """
24                res[_idx % _len] = val
25            stack.append([idx, val])
26        return res
27
28# V0'
29# IDEA : STACK + circular loop handling
30class Solution:
31    def nextGreaterElements(self, nums):
32        ### NOTE : since we can search nums circurly, 
33        #  -> so here we make a new array (augLst = nums + nums) for that     
34        augLst = nums + nums
35        stack = []
36        # init ans
37        res = [-1] * len(nums)
38        ### NOTE : we looping augLst with inverse order
39        for i in range(len(augLst)-1, -1, -1):
40            ### NOTE : if stack and last value in stack smaller than augLst[i], we pop last value from stack
41            while stack and stack[-1] <= augLst[i]:
42                stack.pop()
43            ### NOTE : the remaining element in stack must fit the condition, so we append it to res
44            #   -> note : append to `i % len(nums)` idx in res
45            if stack:
46                res[i % len(nums)] = stack[-1]
47            ### NOTE : we also need to append augLst[i] to stack
48            stack.append(augLst[i])
49        return res

2-4) Daily Temperatures

1# LC 739. Daily Temperatures
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK
4# DEMO 
5#     ...: T=[73, 74, 75, 71, 69, 72, 76, 73]
6#     ...: s=Solution()
7#     ...: r= s.dailyTemperatures(T)
8#     ...: print(r)
9#     ...: 
10# i : 1, stack : [(73, 0)], res : [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
11# i : 2, stack : [(74, 1)], res : [1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
12# i : 5, stack : [(75, 2), (71, 3), (69, 4)], res : [1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
13# i : 5, stack : [(75, 2), (71, 3)], res : [1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 0, 0]
14# i : 6, stack : [(75, 2), (72, 5)], res : [1, 1, 0, 2, 1, 0, 0, 0]
15# i : 6, stack : [(75, 2)], res : [1, 1, 0, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0]
16# [1, 1, 4, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0]
17class Solution(object):
18    def dailyTemperatures(self, T):
19        N = len(T)
20        stack = []
21        res = [0] * N
22        ### NOTE : we only use 1 for loop in this problem
23        for i, t in enumerate(T):
24            # if stack is not bland and last temp < current tmpe
25            # -> pop the stack (get its temp)
26            # -> and calculate the difference 
27            ### BEWARE "while" op 
28            while stack and stack[-1][0] < t:
29                oi = stack.pop()[1]
30                res[oi] = i - oi
31            # no matter any case, we have to insert current temp into stack anyway
32            # since the result (next higher temp) is decided by the coming temp, rather than current temp 
33            stack.append((t, i))
34        return res

1// java
2// LC 739
3
4// V0
5// IDEA : STACK (MONOTONIC STACK)
6// LC 496
7public int[] dailyTemperatures(int[] temperatures) {
8
9    if (temperatures.length == 1){
10        return temperatures;
11    }
12
13    /**
14     *  Stack :
15     *
16     *   -> cache elements (temperature) that DOESN'T have (NOT found) next warmer temperature yet
17     *   -> structure : stack ([temperature, idx])
18     */
19    Stack<List<Integer>> st = new Stack<>(); // element, idx
20    /** NOTE !!!
21     *
22     *    can't use map, since there will be "duplicated" temperature
23     *   -> which will cause different val has same key (hashMap key)
24     */
25    //Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>(); // {temperature : idx-of-next-warmer-temperature}
26    /**
27     *  NOTE !!!
28     *
29     *   we use nextGreater collect answer,
30     *   -> idx : temperature, val : idx-of-next-warmer-temperature
31     */
32    int[] nextGreater = new int[temperatures.length];
33    Arrays.fill(nextGreater, 0); // idx : temperature, val : idx-of-next-warmer-temperature
34    for (int j = 0; j < temperatures.length; j++){
35        int x = temperatures[j];
36        /**
37         *  NOTE !!!
38         *   1) while loop
39         *   2) stack is NOT empty
40         *   3) cache temperature smaller than current temperature
41         *
42         *   st.peek().get(0) is cached temperature
43         */
44        while (!st.isEmpty() && st.peek().get(0) < x){
45            /**
46             *  st.peek().get(1) is idx
47             *
48             */
49            nextGreater[st.peek().get(1)] = j - st.peek().get(1);
50            st.pop();
51        }
52        List<Integer> cur = new ArrayList<>();
53        cur.add(x); // element
54        cur.add(j); // idx
55        st.add(cur);
56    }
57
58    //System.out.println("nextGreater = " + nextGreater);
59    return nextGreater;
60}

2-5) Basic Calculator I

1# LC 224 Basic Calculator
2# V0'
3# IDEA : STACK
4# https://leetcode.com/problems/basic-calculator/solution/
5class Solution:
6    def calculate(self, s):
7
8        stack = []
9        operand = 0
10        res = 0 # For the on-going result
11        sign = 1 # 1 means positive, -1 means negative  
12
13        for ch in s:
14            if ch.isdigit():
15
16                # Forming operand, since it could be more than one digit
17                operand = (operand * 10) + int(ch)
18
19            elif ch == '+':
20
21                # Evaluate the expression to the left,
22                # with result, sign, operand
23                res += sign * operand
24
25                # Save the recently encountered '+' sign
26                sign = 1
27
28                # Reset operand
29                operand = 0
30
31            elif ch == '-':
32
33                res += sign * operand
34                sign = -1
35                operand = 0
36
37            elif ch == '(':
38
39                # Push the result and sign on to the stack, for later
40                # We push the result first, then sign
41                stack.append(res)
42                stack.append(sign)
43
44                # Reset operand and result, as if new evaluation begins for the new sub-expression
45                sign = 1
46                res = 0
47
48            elif ch == ')':
49
50                # Evaluate the expression to the left
51                # with result, sign and operand
52                res += sign * operand
53
54                # ')' marks end of expression within a set of parenthesis
55                # Its result is multiplied with sign on top of stack
56                # as stack.pop() is the sign before the parenthesis
57                res *= stack.pop() # stack pop 1, sign
58
59                # Then add to the next operand on the top.
60                # as stack.pop() is the result calculated before this parenthesis
61                # (operand on stack) + (sign on stack * (result from parenthesis))
62                res += stack.pop() # stack pop 2, operand
63
64                # Reset the operand
65                operand = 0
66
67        return res + sign * operand

2-5’) Basic Calculator II

1# python
2# LC 227. Basic Calculator II, LC 224. Basic Calculator
3# V0
4# IDEA : STACK
5class Solution:
6    def calculate(self, s):
7        stack = []
8        # NOTE THIS !!
9        pre_op = '+'
10        num = 0
11        for i, each in enumerate(s):
12            # case 1) : digit
13            if each.isdigit():
14                num = 10 * num + int(each)  # the way to deal with number like "100", "10"... 
15            if i == len(s) - 1 or each in '+-*/':
16                """
17                NOTE !!! : we deal with "pre_op" (rather than current op)
18                """
19                # case 2) : "+"
20                if pre_op == '+':
21                    stack.append(num)
22                # case 3) : "-"    
23                elif pre_op == '-':
24                    stack.append(-num)
25                # case 3) : "*" 
26                elif pre_op == '*':
27                    stack.append(stack.pop() * num)
28                # case 3) : "/" 
29                elif pre_op == '/':
30                    top = stack.pop()
31                    if top < 0:
32                        stack.append(int(top / num))
33                    else:
34                        stack.append(top // num)
35                # NOTE this!
36                pre_op = each
37                num = 0
38        return sum(stack)
39
40# Algorithm book (labu) p. 342
41# IDEA : STACK + RECURSIVE
42# TODO : fix output format
43class Solution(object):
44    def calculate(self, s):
45
46        def helper(s):
47            stack = []
48            sign = '+'
49            num = 0
50
51            while len(s) > 0:
52                c = s.pop(0)
53                if c.isdigit():
54                    num = 10 * num + int(c)
55                    """
56                    do recursive when meet "("
57                    """
58                    if c == '(':
59                        num = helper(s)
60                    if (not c.isdigit() and c != ' ') or len(s) == 0:
61                        if sign == '+':
62                            stack.append(num)
63                        elif sign == '-':
64                            stack.append(-num)
65                        elif sign == '*':
66                            stack[-1] = stack[-1] * num
67                        elif sign == '/':
68                            stack[-1] = int(stack[-1] / float(num))
69                        num = 0
70                        sign = c
71                    """
72                    end recursive when meet ")"
73                    """
74                    if c == ')':
75                        break
76            return sum(stack)
77
78        # run the helper func    
79        return helper(list(s))
80
81# V1
82# python 3
83class Solution:
84    def calculate(self, s):
85        stack = []
86        pre_op = '+'
87        num = 0
88        for i, each in enumerate(s):
89            if each.isdigit():
90                num = 10 * num + int(each)  # the way to deal with number like "100", "10"... 
91            if i == len(s) - 1 or each in '+-*/':
92                if pre_op == '+':
93                    stack.append(num)
94                elif pre_op == '-':
95                    stack.append(-num)
96                elif pre_op == '*':
97                    stack.append(stack.pop() * num)
98                elif pre_op == '/':
99                    top = stack.pop()
100                    if top < 0:
101                        stack.append(int(top / num))
102                    else:
103                        stack.append(top // num)
104                pre_op = each
105                num = 0
106        return sum(stack)

2-5) Sum of Subarray Minimums

1# LC 907. Sum of Subarray Minimums
2# V0
3# IDEA :  increasing stacks
4class Solution:
5    def sumSubarrayMins(self, A):
6        n, mod = len(A), 10**9 + 7
7        left, right, s1, s2 = [0] * n, [0] * n, [], []
8
9        for i in range(n):
10            count = 1
11            while s1 and s1[-1][0] > A[i]:
12                count += s1.pop()[1]
13            left[i] = count
14            s1.append([A[i], count])
15
16        for i in range(n)[::-1]:
17            count = 1
18            while s2 and s2[-1][0] >= A[i]:
19                count += s2.pop()[1]
20            right[i] = count
21            s2.append([A[i], count])
22        return sum(a * l * r for a, l, r in zip(A, left, right)) % mod

2-6) Asteroid Collision

1# LC 735. Asteroid Collision
2# V0
3class Solution(object):
4    def asteroidCollision(self, asteroids):
5        ans = []
6        for new in asteroids:
7            while ans and new < 0 < ans[-1]:
8                if ans[-1] < -new:
9                    ans.pop()
10                    continue
11                elif ans[-1] == -new:
12                    ans.pop()
13                break
14            else:
15                ans.append(new)
16        return ans

2-7) Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String

1# LC 1047. Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK
4class Solution:
5     def removeDuplicates(self, x):
6          # edge
7          if not x:
8            return
9          stack = []
10          """
11          NOTE !!! below op
12          """
13          for i in range(len(x)):
14               # NOTE !!! : trick here : if stack last element == current x's element
15               #       -> we pop last stack element
16               #       -> and NOT add current element
17               if stack and stack[-1] == x[i]:
18                    stack.pop(-1)
19               # if stack last element != current x's element
20               #      -> we append x[i]
21               else:
22                    stack.append(x[i])
23          return "".join(stack)
24
25# V0'
26# IDEA : TWO POINTERS
27#      -> pointers : end, c
28class Solution:
29     def removeDuplicates(self, S):
30            end =  -1
31            a = list(S)
32            for c in a:
33                if end >= 0 and a[end] == c:
34                    end -= 1
35                else:
36                    end += 1
37                    a[end] = c
38            return ''.join(a[: end + 1])

2-8) Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II

Pattern: Stack with Character-Count Pairs

This pattern uses Stack<int[]> or Stack<[char, count]> to efficiently track consecutive duplicates and their counts. It’s particularly useful when you need to remove k consecutive equal elements.

When to Use This Pattern:

  1. Problem mentions “k consecutive/adjacent equal elements”

    • Remove k duplicates: LC 1209
    • Count k consecutive: various counting problems

  2. Need to track both character AND its frequency

    • Can’t just track character (need count for k-removal)
    • Can’t just track count (need to know which character)

  3. Removal happens when count reaches threshold k

    • Unlike LC 1047 (k=2, simple stack.pop()), k is variable
    • Need to track partial progress (e.g., “aa” in “aaab” with k=3)

  4. One-pass solution required with O(n) space

    • Stack stores compressed form: {char, count}
    • More efficient than storing all characters

Recognition Signs:

  • ✓ Keywords: “k adjacent”, “k consecutive”, “k duplicates”
  • ✓ Remove/count when reaching exactly k occurrences
  • ✓ Need to handle partial sequences (count < k)
  • ✓ Input constraint: k >= 2 (if k=1, different approach needed)

Structure:

1// Core data structure
2Stack<int[]> stack = new Stack<>();
3// Each element: {character_as_int, count}
4
5// Or for clarity
6Stack<Pair<Character, Integer>> stack = new Stack<>();

Similar Problems:

  • LC 1047: Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String (k=2 special case)
  • LC 1544: Make The String Great (remove adjacent opposite case)
  • LC 316: Remove Duplicate Letters (lexicographical order with stack)
  • LC 394: Decode String (stack with count, but for repetition)
1# LC 1209. Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK
4class Solution:
5     def removeDuplicates(self, x, k):
6          # edge case
7          if not x:
8            return None
9          stack = []
10          """
11          NOTE !!!
12            1) we use [[element, _count]] format for below op
13            2) note the case when deal with duplicated elements
14
15               if stack and stack[-1][0] == x[i]:
16                    if stack[-1][1] < k-1:
17                         stack[-1][1] += 1
18                    else:
19                         stack.pop(-1)
20          """
21          for i in range(len(x)):
22               if stack and stack[-1][0] == x[i]:
23                    if stack[-1][1] < k-1:
24                         stack[-1][1] += 1
25                    else:
26                         stack.pop(-1)
27               else:
28                    stack.append([x[i], 1])
29          #print (">> stack = " + str(stack))
30          tmp = [x[0]*x[1] for x in stack]
31          #print (">> tmp = " + str(tmp))
32          return "".join(tmp)
33
34# V0'
35# IDEA : STACK
36# NOTE !!! we DON'T need to modify original s, (but maintain an extra stack for duplicated checks)
37class Solution:
38     def removeDuplicates(self, s, k):
39            stack = [['#', 0]]
40            for c in s:
41                #print ("c = " + str(c) + " stack = " + str(stack))
42                if stack[-1][0] == c:
43                    stack[-1][1] += 1
44                    if stack[-1][1] == k:
45                        stack.pop()
46                else:
47                    stack.append([c, 1])
48            return ''.join(c * k for c, k in stack)

1// java
2// LC 1209. Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II
3
4/**
5 * Problem: Remove k consecutive equal characters repeatedly until no more removals possible.
6 *
7 * Examples:
8 * - s = "deeedbbcccbdaa", k = 3
9 *   "deeedbbcccbdaa" → "ddbbbdaa" (remove "eee", "ccc")
10 *   "ddbbbdaa" → "dddaa" (remove "bbb")
11 *   "dddaa" → "aa" (remove "ddd")
12 *
13 * - s = "pbbcggttciiippooaais", k = 2
14 *   Output: "ps"
15 *
16 * Key Insight:
17 * - Use Stack<int[]> to store {character, count} pairs
18 * - When char matches stack top: increment count
19 * - When count reaches k: pop (remove k consecutive chars)
20 * - This handles cascading removals naturally
21 *
22 * Time: O(N) - single pass through string
23 * Space: O(N) - worst case all different characters
24 */
25
26// V0
27// IDEA: STACK with {char, count} pairs
28/**
29 * time = O(N)
30 * space = O(N)
31 */
32public String removeDuplicates(String s, int k) {
33    if (s == null || s.length() == 0 || k <= 0) {
34        return s;
35    }
36
37    /**
38     * NOTE !!!
39     * Stack stores int array: {character_as_int, count}
40     *
41     * Why int[] instead of Pair<Character, Integer>?
42     * - More memory efficient (no object wrapper overhead)
43     * - Direct access: pair[0] = char, pair[1] = count
44     * - Java doesn't have built-in Pair in older versions
45     */
46    Stack<int[]> st = new Stack<>();
47
48    for (char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
49        /**
50         * Case 1: Character matches stack top
51         * Increment the count of consecutive occurrences
52         */
53        if (!st.isEmpty() && st.peek()[0] == ch) {
54            st.peek()[1]++;
55
56            /**
57             * NOTE !!!
58             * When count reaches k, remove the entire block
59             * This triggers potential cascading removals
60             */
61            if (st.peek()[1] == k) {
62                st.pop();
63            }
64        }
65        /**
66         * Case 2: New character (different from stack top)
67         * Start a new block with count = 1
68         */
69        else {
70            st.push(new int[] { ch, 1 });
71        }
72    }
73
74    /**
75     * Build final string from remaining characters in stack
76     * Each stack element may have count > 1
77     */
78    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
79    for (int[] pair : st) {
80        char c = (char) pair[0];
81        int count = pair[1];
82
83        // Append character 'count' times
84        for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
85            sb.append(c);
86        }
87    }
88
89    return sb.toString();
90}
91
92/**
93 * Example Walkthrough: s = "deeedbbcccbdaa", k = 3
94 *
95 * Iteration:
96 * ch='d': st = [{d,1}]
97 * ch='e': st = [{d,1}, {e,1}]
98 * ch='e': st = [{d,1}, {e,2}]
99 * ch='e': st = [{d,1}, {e,3}] → count==k, pop → st = [{d,1}]
100 * ch='d': st = [{d,2}]
101 * ch='b': st = [{d,2}, {b,1}]
102 * ch='b': st = [{d,2}, {b,2}]
103 * ch='c': st = [{d,2}, {b,2}, {c,1}]
104 * ch='c': st = [{d,2}, {b,2}, {c,2}]
105 * ch='c': st = [{d,2}, {b,2}, {c,3}] → pop → st = [{d,2}, {b,2}]
106 * ch='b': st = [{d,2}, {b,3}] → pop → st = [{d,2}]
107 * ch='d': st = [{d,3}] → pop → st = []
108 * ch='a': st = [{a,1}]
109 * ch='a': st = [{a,2}]
110 *
111 * Result: "aa"
112 */
113
114// V1
115// IDEA: Two separate stacks (cleaner type safety)
116/**
117 * time = O(N)
118 * space = O(N)
119 */
120public String removeDuplicates_v1(String s, int k) {
121    Stack<Character> charStack = new Stack<>();
122    Stack<Integer> countStack = new Stack<>();
123
124    for (char ch : s.toCharArray()) {
125        if (!charStack.isEmpty() && charStack.peek() == ch) {
126            countStack.push(countStack.peek() + 1);
127        } else {
128            countStack.push(1);
129        }
130
131        charStack.push(ch);
132
133        // Remove k characters when count reaches k
134        if (countStack.peek() == k) {
135            for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
136                charStack.pop();
137                countStack.pop();
138            }
139        }
140    }
141
142    // Build result
143    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
144    while (!charStack.isEmpty()) {
145        sb.append(charStack.pop());
146    }
147
148    return sb.reverse().toString();
149}
150
151/**
152 * Common Mistakes:
153 *
154 * 1. Forgetting to check !st.isEmpty() before peek()
155 *    ✗ if (st.peek()[0] == ch)  // NPE if stack empty!
156 *    ✓ if (!st.isEmpty() && st.peek()[0] == ch)
157 *
158 * 2. Removing only one character instead of the whole block
159 *    ✗ if (st.peek()[1] == k) st.peek()[1] = 0;  // Wrong!
160 *    ✓ if (st.peek()[1] == k) st.pop();
161 *
162 * 3. Not handling count correctly when building result
163 *    ✗ sb.append((char) pair[0]);  // Only appends once!
164 *    ✓ for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) sb.append(c);
165 *
166 * 4. Using wrong data structure (List instead of Stack)
167 *    ✗ List doesn't support peek() efficiently
168 *    ✓ Stack or Deque for O(1) peek/pop
169 */
170
171/**
172 * Interview Tips:
173 *
174 * 1. Clarify edge cases:
175 *    - What if k > s.length()? (no removal possible)
176 *    - What if k == 1? (all characters removed)
177 *    - Empty string input?
178 *
179 * 2. Discuss trade-offs:
180 *    - Stack<int[]> vs two separate stacks
181 *      • int[]: more memory efficient, less readable
182 *      • Two stacks: cleaner, type-safe, slightly more space
183 *
184 * 3. Follow-up optimizations:
185 *    - Can we do it in-place? (tricky, but possible with two pointers)
186 *    - What if k is very large? (same approach works)
187 *    - What if we need to track which removals were made? (add to result list)
188 *
189 * 4. Related patterns:
190 *    - LC 1047 (k=2): simpler, can use single stack
191 *    - LC 394 (Decode String): similar stack with count pattern
192 *    - LC 316 (Remove Duplicate Letters): stack with different removal criteria
193 */

2-9) Simplify Path

1# LC 71. Simplify Path
2
3# V0
4# IDEA : STACK
5class Solution:
6    def simplifyPath(self, path: str) -> str:
7        s = path.split('/')
8        result = []
9        for i in range(len(s)):
10            if s[i] and s[i] != '.' and s[i]!='/' and s[i]!='..':
11                result.append(s[i])
12            elif s[i] == '..':
13                if result:
14                    result.pop()
15        
16        return "/"+"/".join(result)

2-10) Min Stack

1# LC 155. Min Stack
2# V0
3# IDEA : STACK
4# IDEA : 
5# -> USE A STACK TO STORAGE MIN VALUE IN THE STACK WHEN EVERY PUSH
6# -> SO WE CAN RETURN getMin IN CONSTANT TIEM VIA STACK ABOVE
7class MinStack(object):
8
9    def __init__(self):
10        """
11        initialize your data structure here.
12        """
13        self.stack = []
14        
15    def push(self, x):
16        if not self.stack:
17            """
18            NOTE : we use stack = [(x, y)]
19                    x is the current element
20                    y in current MIN value in current stack
21            """
22            ### note here
23            self.stack.append((x, x))
24        ### NOTICE HERE 
25        # stack[i][1] save to min value when every push
26        # so the latest min in stack is at stack[-1][1]
27        else:
28            ### note here
29            self.stack.append((x, min(x, self.stack[-1][1])))
30        
31    def pop(self):
32        self.stack.pop()
33        
34    def top(self):
35        return self.stack[-1][0]
36        
37    def getMin(self):
38        # the latest min in stack is at stack[-1][1]
39        return self.stack[-1][1]

2-11) Sum of Subarray Ranges

1# LC 2104. Sum of Subarray Ranges
2# NOTE : there are also brute force, 2 pointers ... approaches
3# V0'
4# IDEA : monotonic stack
5# https://zhuanlan.zhihu.com/p/444725220
6class Solution:
7    def subArrayRanges(self, nums):
8        A, s, res = [-float('inf')] + nums + [-float('inf')], [], 0
9        for i, num in enumerate(A):
10            while s and num < A[s[-1]]:
11                j = s.pop()
12                res -= (i - j) * (j - s[-1]) * A[j]
13            s.append(i)
14        A, s = [float('inf')] + nums + [float('inf')], []
15        for i, num in enumerate(A):
16            while s and num > A[s[-1]]:
17                j = s.pop()
18                res += (i - j) * (j - s[-1]) * A[j]
19            s.append(i)
20        return res

2-11) Largest Rectangle in Histogram

1# LC 84. Largest Rectangle in Histogram
2# python
3# V1'''
4# IDEA : STACK
5# https://leetcode.com/problems/largest-rectangle-in-histogram/solution/
6class Solution:
7    def largestRectangleArea(self, heights: List[int]) -> int:
8        stack = [-1]
9        max_area = 0
10        for i in range(len(heights)):
11            while stack[-1] != -1 and heights[stack[-1]] >= heights[i]:
12                current_height = heights[stack.pop()]
13                current_width = i - stack[-1] - 1
14                max_area = max(max_area, current_height * current_width)
15            stack.append(i)
16
17        while stack[-1] != -1:
18            current_height = heights[stack.pop()]
19            current_width = len(heights) - stack[-1] - 1
20            max_area = max(max_area, current_height * current_width)
21        return max_area

2-12) Remove Duplicate Letters

1// java
2// LC 316
3
4/**
5*  NOTE
6*
7*  Lexicographically Smaller
8*
9* A string a is lexicographically smaller than a
10* string b if in the first position where a and b differ,
11* string a has a letter that appears earlier in the alphabet
12* than the corresponding letter in b.
13* If the first min(a.length, b.length) characters do not differ,
14* then the shorter string is the lexicographically smaller one.
15*
16*/
17
18// V0-1
19// IDEA: STACK (fixed by gpt)
20// Time: O(n) — one pass over the string and each character is pushed/popped at most once.
21// Space: O(1) — constant space for 26 characters (seen, freq, stack)
22/**
23* 📌 Example Walkthrough
24*
25* Input: "cbacdcbc"
26*    1.  'c' → Stack: ["c"]
27*    2.  'b' < 'c' and 'c' still appears → pop 'c', push 'b'
28*    3.  'a' < 'b' → pop 'b', push 'a'
29*    4.  'c' > 'a' → push 'c'
30*    5.  'd' > 'c' → push 'd'
31*    6.  'c' already seen → skip
32*    7.  'b' > 'd' → push 'b'
33*    8.  'c' > 'b' → push 'c'
34*
35* Final stack: ['a', 'c', 'd', 'b']
36* Lexicographically smallest valid string: "acdb"
37*
38*/
39public String removeDuplicateLetters_0_1(String s) {
40  if (s == null || s.length() == 0) {
41      return "";
42  }
43
44/**
45 *  •   freq: array to count how many times each letter appears in s.
46 *  •   We use c - 'a' to map each character to index 0–25 ('a' to 'z').
47 *  •   This helps us later determine if we can remove a character and see it again later.
48 */
49int[] freq = new int[26]; // frequency of each character
50  for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
51      freq[c - 'a']++;
52  }
53
54/**
55 *  •   Tracks which characters have already been added to the result.
56 *  •   This ensures we only include each character once.
57 *
58 *
59 *  NOTE !!! sean is a `boolean` array
60 */
61boolean[] seen = new boolean[26]; // whether character is in stack/result
62
63/** NOTE !!!
64 *
65 *  we init stack here
66 *
67 *
68 *  •   This stack is used to build the final result.
69 *  •   We’ll maintain characters in order and manipulate
70 *      the top to maintain lexicographical order.
71 */
72/**
73 *  NOTE !!!
74 *
75 *   use `STACK`, but NOT use `PQ`
76 *
77 */
78Stack<Character> stack = new Stack<>();
79
80/**
81 *  •   Iterate through the string one character at a time.
82 *  •   Since we’ve now processed c, decrement its frequency count.
83 */
84for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
85      freq[c - 'a']--; // reduce frequency, since we're processing this char
86
87    /**
88     *  •   If we’ve already added this character to the result,
89     *      skip it — we only want one occurrence of each letter.
90     */
91      if (seen[c - 'a']) {
92          continue; // already added, skip
93      }
94
95  /** NOTE !!!
96   *
97   * Now we’re checking:
98   *
99   *    •   Is the stack NOT empty?
100   *
101   *    •   Is the current character c lexicographically
102   *        smaller than the character at the top of the stack?
103   *
104   *    •   Does the character at the top of the stack still
105   *        appear later (i.e., its freq > 0)?
106   *
107   * If yes to all, we can:
108   *
109   *    •   pop it from the result,
110   *
111   *    •   and add it later again in a better
112   *        position (lexicographically smaller order).
113   */
114  // remove characters that are bigger than current AND appear later again
115  while (!stack.isEmpty() && c < stack.peek() && freq[stack.peek() - 'a'] > 0) {
116          /**
117           *
118           *    Remove the character from the stack,
119           *    and mark it as not seen so it can be added again later.
120           */
121          char removed = stack.pop();
122          seen[removed - 'a'] = false;
123      }
124
125  /**
126   *    •   Push the current character c to the stack,
127   *    •   And mark it as seen (i.e., already in the result).
128   */
129  stack.push(c);
130  seen[c - 'a'] = true;
131  }
132
133  // build result from stack
134  StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
135  for (char c : stack) {
136      sb.append(c);
137  }
138
139  return sb.toString();
140}

2-13) Remove K Digits

1// java
2// LC 402. Remove K Digits
3
4/**
5 * Problem: Given a non-negative integer num and an integer k,
6 * return the smallest possible integer after removing k digits from num.
7 *
8 * Key Insight:
9 * To make the number as small as possible, we want smaller digits
10 * at the beginning (most significant positions).
11 *
12 * Greedy Strategy:
13 * - Use a monotonic increasing stack
14 * - If current digit is smaller than stack top, pop the larger digit
15 * - Continue popping while k > 0 and current digit < stack top
16 * - This ensures we remove larger digits from higher positions
17 *
18 * Time: O(N) - each digit pushed/popped at most once
19 * Space: O(N) - stack size
20 */
21
22// V0-1
23// IDEA: MONOTONIC STACK (increasing)
24public String removeKdigits(String num, int k) {
25    int n = num.length();
26    if (k == n)
27        return "0";
28
29    // Use Deque as stack for efficient operations
30    Deque<Character> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
31
32    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
33        char digit = num.charAt(i);
34
35        /**
36         * NOTE !!!
37         * While we can still remove digits (k > 0)
38         * and current digit is smaller than stack top,
39         * pop the stack (greedy removal of larger digits)
40         */
41        while (k > 0 && !stack.isEmpty() && stack.peekLast() > digit) {
42            stack.removeLast();
43            k--;
44        }
45        stack.addLast(digit);
46    }
47
48    // Edge case: if k > 0, remove digits from end (e.g., "1111")
49    while (k > 0) {
50        stack.removeLast();
51        k--;
52    }
53
54    // Build result and remove leading zeros
55    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
56    boolean leadingZero = true;
57    while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
58        char c = stack.removeFirst();
59        if (leadingZero && c == '0')
60            continue;
61        leadingZero = false;
62        sb.append(c);
63    }
64
65    return sb.length() == 0 ? "0" : sb.toString();
66}
67
68/**
69 * Example Walkthrough:
70 *
71 * Input: num = "1432219", k = 3
72 *
73 * Step-by-step:
74 * 1. Push '1': [1]
75 * 2. Push '4': [1, 4]
76 * 3. '3' < '4': Pop '4', push '3', k=2. Stack: [1, 3]
77 * 4. '2' < '3': Pop '3', push '2', k=1. Stack: [1, 2]
78 * 5. Push '2': [1, 2, 2]
79 * 6. '1' < '2': Pop '2', push '1', k=0. Stack: [1, 2, 1]
80 * 7. k=0, push '9': [1, 2, 1, 9]
81 *
82 * Result: "1219"
83 *
84 * Why ArrayDeque?
85 * - Stack<Character> is synchronized and slow
86 * - ArrayDeque is faster and modern alternative for stack operations
87 */

2-14) Online Stock Span

1// java
2// LC 901. Online Stock Span
3
4/**
5 * Problem: Design an algorithm that collects daily price quotes for some stock
6 * and returns the span of that stock's price for the current day.
7 *
8 * The span is the maximum number of consecutive days (starting from today and going backward)
9 * for which the stock price was less than or equal to today's price.
10 *
11 * Example:
12 * Prices: [100, 80, 60, 70, 60, 75, 85]
13 * Spans:  [1,   1,  1,  2,  1,  4,  6]
14 *
15 * Key Insight:
16 * - Use monotonic decreasing stack to track [price, span] pairs
17 * - When new price arrives, pop all smaller/equal prices
18 * - Accumulate their spans into current span
19 * - This gives us the count of consecutive days with price <= current
20 *
21 * Time: O(1) amortized per next() call (each element pushed/popped once)
22 * Space: O(N) for the stack
23 */
24
25// V0
26// IDEA: MONOTONIC STACK (decreasing) + SPAN ACCUMULATION
27class StockSpanner {
28
29    /**
30     * NOTE !!!
31     * Stack stores [price, span] pairs
32     * - price: the stock price
33     * - span: how many consecutive days (including itself) had price <= this price
34     */
35    private Deque<int[]> stack; // {price, span}
36
37    public StockSpanner() {
38        stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
39    }
40
41    /**
42     * NOTE !!!
43     * Monotonic decreasing stack pattern:
44     * 1. Start with span = 1 (today counts)
45     * 2. While stack top has price <= current price:
46     *    - Pop it and add its span to current span
47     * 3. Push [current price, accumulated span]
48     * 4. Return span
49     */
50    public int next(int price) {
51        int span = 1; // Today always counts as 1
52
53        /**
54         * Pop all prices that are less than or equal to current price
55         * and accumulate their spans
56         */
57        while (!stack.isEmpty() && stack.peek()[0] <= price) {
58            // "Absorb" the previous span into current span
59            span += stack.pop()[1];
60        }
61
62        // Push current price with its accumulated span
63        stack.push(new int[] { price, span });
64
65        return span;
66    }
67}
68
69/**
70 * Example Walkthrough:
71 *
72 * Input: [100, 80, 60, 70, 60, 75, 85]
73 *
74 * next(100):
75 *   - span = 1, stack is empty
76 *   - Push [100, 1]
77 *   - Return 1
78 *   Stack: [[100, 1]]
79 *
80 * next(80):
81 *   - span = 1, stack top is [100, 1], 100 > 80, don't pop
82 *   - Push [80, 1]
83 *   - Return 1
84 *   Stack: [[80, 1], [100, 1]]
85 *
86 * next(60):
87 *   - span = 1, stack top is [80, 1], 80 > 60, don't pop
88 *   - Push [60, 1]
89 *   - Return 1
90 *   Stack: [[60, 1], [80, 1], [100, 1]]
91 *
92 * next(70):
93 *   - span = 1
94 *   - stack top is [60, 1], 60 <= 70, pop and add span: span = 1 + 1 = 2
95 *   - stack top is [80, 1], 80 > 70, stop
96 *   - Push [70, 2]
97 *   - Return 2
98 *   Stack: [[70, 2], [80, 1], [100, 1]]
99 *
100 * next(60):
101 *   - span = 1
102 *   - stack top is [70, 2], 70 > 60, don't pop
103 *   - Push [60, 1]
104 *   - Return 1
105 *   Stack: [[60, 1], [70, 2], [80, 1], [100, 1]]
106 *
107 * next(75):
108 *   - span = 1
109 *   - stack top is [60, 1], 60 <= 75, pop and add: span = 1 + 1 = 2
110 *   - stack top is [70, 2], 70 <= 75, pop and add: span = 2 + 2 = 4
111 *   - stack top is [80, 1], 80 > 75, stop
112 *   - Push [75, 4]
113 *   - Return 4 (covers prices: 60, 70, 60, 75)
114 *   Stack: [[75, 4], [80, 1], [100, 1]]
115 *
116 * next(85):
117 *   - span = 1
118 *   - stack top is [75, 4], 75 <= 85, pop and add: span = 1 + 4 = 5
119 *   - stack top is [80, 1], 80 <= 85, pop and add: span = 5 + 1 = 6
120 *   - stack top is [100, 1], 100 > 85, stop
121 *   - Push [85, 6]
122 *   - Return 6 (covers prices: 60, 70, 60, 75, 80, 85)
123 *   Stack: [[85, 6], [100, 1]]
124 *
125 * Why this works:
126 * - When we pop [60, 1] and [70, 2], we're saying:
127 *   "60 had 1 consecutive day <= 60 (itself)"
128 *   "70 had 2 consecutive days <= 70 (60, 70)"
129 * - By accumulating: span = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4
130 *   We get: "75 has 4 consecutive days <= 75 (60, 70, 60, 75)"
131 */
132
133/**
134 * Usage:
135 * StockSpanner obj = new StockSpanner();
136 * int span = obj.next(price);
137 */
